content.text: Prefilling for JSON
is a prompt engineering technique where you
inject the start of Claude's response
{"issues": [
) to bypass friendly preambles and enforce your JSON structure.
What Prefilling for JSON Is
Aspect
Description
Purpose
Force Claude to start with your JSON format, not "Certainly! Here's the JSON..."
Mechanism
Set the first
assistant
turn to the opening of your JSON structure
Result
Claude completes from that point — can't escape the format you started
Reliability
"Most reliable structured output technique" without structured outputs API
How to Prefill for JSON

# Without prefill (problematic)
messages=[
    {"role": "user", "content": "Return issues as JSON"}
]
# Claude might respond: "Certainly! Here's your JSON:\n{\"issues\": [...]}"

# With prefill (reliable)
messages=[
    {"role": "user", "content": "Return issues as JSON"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": '{"issues": ['}  # Prefill the opening
]
# Claude completes: '{"query": "example"}, {"query": "test"}]'
Key Takeaways from Prefilling
No "Certainly!" or extraneous text
— Prefill bypasses friendly preamble
JSON structure more consistent
— Claude completes from your structure
Combine with stop sequences
— Ensures no extra content at end of JSON
Most reliable technique
— "It can't escape the format you've started"
Prefilling vs Structured Outputs
Feature
Prefilling (Prompt Engineering)
Structured Outputs (API)
Guarantee
High reliability but not 100%
100% schema compliance
Mechanism
Trick/pattern
Constrained decoding
Use case
General output consistency
Guaranteed JSON schema conformance
Recommendation
Use when structured outputs unavailable
Use for guaranteed schema
Important:
If you need Claude to always output valid JSON conforming to a schema,
use Structured Outputs instead of prefilling
When Prefilling Works Well
✅ Simple JSON structures (1-2 levels deep)
✅ No code fences — Claude loves wrapping JSON in backticks unless told not to
✅ Combined with stop sequences for clean endings
When Prefilling Breaks
Nesting 3+ levels deep
— "Claude starts drifting no matter how clean your schema"
❌ Complex schemas with many constraints
❌ Without hard token caps — you get "valid JSON that's bloated and useless"
Complete Pattern: Prefill + Stop Sequence

# Best prefill pattern
messages=[
    {"role": "user", "content": "Return issues as JSON. No code fences."},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": '{"issues": ['}  # Prefill opening
]

# Optional: Add stop sequence to prevent trailing content
stop_sequences=['\n}', '```']  # Stop before closing braces or markdown
Prefilling Limitations in Modern Claude
Important:
Prefilling is
not supported
on several newer Claude models:
❌ Claude Fable 5
❌ Claude Mythos 5
❌ Claude Mythos Preview
❌ Claude Opus 4.8, 4.7, 4.6
❌ Claude Sonnet 4.6 []
For these models:
Use structured outputs or system prompt instructions instead [].
Prefilling vs Other Reliability Techniques
Technique
Reliability
Best For
Prefill
High (pre-structured outputs)
JSON format enforcement []
Schema-first prompting
Medium-High
Exact schema instructions []
Examples > Instructions
High
Showing format vs describing []
Retry contract
Medium
Error handling patterns []
Structured Outputs API
Guaranteed schema compliance
Best Practices for Prefilling
Start with
— Prefill the opening brace to lock into object format []
Say "No code fences"
— Prevent markdown backtick wrapping []
Add stop sequences
— Prevent extra trailing content []
Use examples
— One example beats three paragraphs of format instructions []
Keep token caps
— Prevent bloated valid JSON []
Bottom Line: Use Structured Outputs Instead
Prefilling for JSON is a legacy technique
— Modern Claude API provides
Structured Outputs
with
guaranteed schema compliance
via constrained decoding [][].
When to still use prefilling:

- Using older Claude models without structured outputs support
- General output consistency (not strict schema) []
- Quick prototyping before API integration
When to use Structured Outputs:

- Need guaranteed JSON schema conformance []
- Production systems requiring 100% reliability
- Any computer-to-computer interface
How It Works in Practice
Understanding the internals of prefilling for json helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Best Practices
Production experience with prefilling for json has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Performance Benchmarks and Cost Analysis
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Security Considerations
Deploying prefilling for json in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.
Advanced Best Practices

Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.

Advanced Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.

Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Performance Benchmarks and Cost Analysis
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.

Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Security Considerations
Deploying prefilling for json in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.

Best Practices
Following best practices ensures effective use of Prefilling for JSON in real-world applications.
Start with the simplest approach that solves your problem before adding complexity.
Test edge cases thoroughly, especially boundary conditions and error states.
Document your implementation decisions for future maintainability.
Monitor performance metrics and optimize based on actual usage patterns.
Follow security best practices and validate all inputs at system boundaries.
Use version control and code reviews for all production changes.
Establish clear error handling strategies before deployment.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Understanding typical pitfalls in Prefilling for JSON helps you avoid costly errors.
Over-engineering solutions when simpler approaches would suffice — start minimal.
Neglecting error handling, leading to silent failures in production.
Skipping testing because the implementation appears straightforward.
Ignoring rate limits and quotas when designing at scale.
Not considering the user experience impact of latency or error states.
Performance and Optimization
Optimizing Prefilling for JSON for production environments requires attention to key performance factors.
Factor
Impact
Optimization Strategy
Latency
High
Use caching and streaming responses
Throughput
Medium
Implement request batching and connection pooling
Cost
High
Monitor token usage and optimize prompt length
Reliability
Critical
Add retries with exponential backoff
Security Considerations
Security is paramount when implementing Prefilling for JSON in production systems.
Never expose API keys in client-side code or public repositories.
Validate and sanitize all user inputs before processing.
Implement proper authentication and authorization controls.
Audit logs for all sensitive operations and data access.
Follow the principle of least privilege for all service accounts.
Summary

This section covered Prefilling for JSON in depth, including core concepts, practical implementation approaches, and production considerations. Apply these patterns consistently to build robust, maintainable systems.
What Prefilling Is and Why It Works

Prefilling is the technique of providing the beginning of Claude's response as the final message in the messages array with role="assistant", so that Claude's actual output continues from that starting point rather than beginning from scratch. When you start the assistant turn with a partial JSON string such as {"result":, Claude is constrained to continue the JSON structure you opened rather than beginning its reply with conversational preamble such as "Sure! Here's the JSON you requested:". This works because the Messages API treats the assistant prefill as already-generated tokens — the model generates the next token given the entire context, and the first plausible continuation of {"result": is a JSON value, not an apology or explanation. Prefilling is one of the most reliable single-technique improvements for JSON generation because it eliminates the ambiguity of the instruction "respond only with JSON" while adding virtually zero complexity to the API call.
Prefilling forces Claude to continue an already-started response rather than choosing how to open its reply.
Starting with { or [" removes the model's option to preface JSON with explanation text, markdown code fences, or preamble.
The technique works at the token level: the model generates the highest-probability continuation of the given prefix.
Prefilling is not a system prompt instruction — it acts as a hard constraint through the message structure itself.
Compatible with all Claude models that accept the messages API format; no special model flag required.
Prefill content is counted as output tokens for billing purposes, so very long prefills add marginal cost.
How to Implement Prefilling in the API

Implementing prefilling requires adding an assistant-role message as the last element of your messages array. The message content is a string containing the start of the JSON you want Claude to complete. The API requires that you do not close the JSON in the prefill — you provide an open structure and Claude closes it. Important: when using the Python SDK, the final message must be role="assistant" and must come after all user messages. The SDK does not require any special parameter — the messages array structure is all that is needed. When Claude responds, its reply begins exactly where your prefill ended; you then concatenate your prefill with Claude's reply to get the complete JSON string. This concatenation step is the only extra logic your application needs to handle.
The prefill message must be role="assistant" and must be the last element in the messages array.
Do not include a system prompt instruction to "return only JSON" — the prefill makes that instruction redundant.
Claude's response begins where your prefill ends; concatenate prefill + response to get the full JSON.
The prefill must be syntactically consistent with your schema — starting with {"items": prevents Claude from returning a flat object.
Prefilling is not supported in all API surfaces: check whether the endpoint or SDK version you are using accepts partial assistant messages.
Test the concatenated result with json.loads() in Python or JSON.parse() in JavaScript to catch malformed output before returning it to callers.
When Prefilling Helps Most

Prefilling delivers its greatest reliability improvement in three specific scenarios. First, when Claude is prone to adding markdown code fences (```json\n{...}\n```) around its JSON response — starting with { bypasses the fence entirely. Second, when the schema is deeply nested and Claude occasionally produces a simplified version when given free rein to start its response — the prefill anchors the structure. Third, when response latency matters: prefilling with the opening of the expected response reduces the probability of a long preamble, cutting time to first useful token. Prefilling is less critical when you are already using tool_use/function calling, because the structured output is channeled through the tool_use input field by the API itself, not through the text content. For strictly structured extraction (names, dates, numbers), prefer tool use over prefilling when your model version supports it.
Eliminates markdown code fences: Claude cannot output ```json before { if the response already starts with {.
Anchors nested schema structure: prefilling the first two keys of a complex object reduces schema deviation.
Reduces time-to-first-useful-token by bypassing conversational preamble generation.
Best for text completion endpoints and workflows where tool_use is unavailable or adds overhead.
Less necessary when using tool_use/function calling: the API channels structured output through tool input fields automatically.
High-value for batch extraction pipelines where parsing failures fail the job — prefilling reduces the retry rate.
Combining Prefilling with System Prompts for Maximum Reliability

Prefilling and system prompts are complementary, not redundant. The system prompt communicates the schema, field names, expected types, and any business rules Claude should apply when generating values. The prefill enforces the structural starting point that makes the system prompt instructions syntactically inescapable. A well-crafted combination looks like: system prompt = "You are a data extraction assistant. Extract the following fields from user input and return them as JSON: {name: string, age: number, email: string}. Return only the JSON object.", followed by a user message with the raw text, followed by an assistant prefill of {"name":. The system prompt tells Claude what fields exist and what types they should be; the prefill forces the response to begin as a JSON object. This layered approach achieves near-100% schema-compliant output on well-formed inputs without post-processing.
System prompt: define the schema, field names, and types — Claude uses this to decide what values to extract or generate.
Prefill: enforce the structural starting point — opening brace, first key, or even the full key set with empty values.
Do not duplicate instructions: if the prefill already forces JSON, the system prompt instruction to "return only JSON" is redundant noise.
Include the schema definition in the system prompt as a JSON Schema object for the most precise field type guidance.
Test the combination on edge cases: empty inputs, malformed inputs, and inputs with all optional fields absent.
Log the full concatenated response (prefill + output) when debugging — the concatenated string is what json.loads() receives.

import json
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a structured data extraction assistant.
Extract the following fields from the user's text and return them as JSON.
Schema:
{
  "name": string,
  "email": string,
  "age": number or null,
  "topics": [string]
}
Return only the JSON object — no explanation, no markdown fences."""

def extract_structured(raw_text: str) -> dict:
    prefill = '{"name": '  # anchor the opening structure

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": raw_text},
            # Prefill: Claude continues from here
            {"role": "assistant", "content": prefill}
        ]
    )

    # Concatenate prefill + Claude's continuation
    full_json_str = prefill + response.content[0].text

    try:
        return json.loads(full_json_str)
    except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
        raise ValueError(f"Claude returned malformed JSON: {e}\nRaw: {full_json_str}") from e

# Example usage
sample = """
Hi, I am Alice Johnson, alice@example.com, 34 years old.
I am interested in machine learning, Python, and distributed systems.
"""

result = extract_structured(sample)
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
# Expected output:
# {
#   "name": "Alice Johnson",
#   "email": "alice@example.com",
#   "age": 34,
#   "topics": ["machine learning", "Python", "distributed systems"]
# }
